message.content:
Research **Directus** (directus.io / directus.com / github.com/directus/directus) with CURRENT (2024-2025) web sources. Fetch official docs, the Directus Marketplace, GitHub, and community projects. Cite URLs. Be honest; say "I could not find" rather than guessing.

CONTEXT: A 9-person company (<$5M revenue, so eligible for Directus's free self-host grant) is deciding whether to build BOTH a CRM and a project-management system on Directus instead of their current forked Twenty CRM. They HATE forking (upgrade pain). The decisive question is: how much can be done by CONFIGURATION + DROP-IN EXTENSIONS (no forking), and what ready-made open-source UI/skins exist so they don't build everything from scratch.

Answer these precisely:

1. **What Directus actually is.** Confirm it's a headless/back-end "data platform" on top of SQL (Postgres/etc.) with an admin "Data Studio" — NOT a project-management tool or CRM out of the box. Describe what the stock Data Studio UI gives an end user (collections, item editor, layouts: table/cards/kanban/calendar/map?, Insights dashboards). Is there a built-in KANBAN layout? Gantt? 

2. **Extensions SDK — enumerate every extension type** and whether each is a clean drop-in (installed via Marketplace/npm, NO core fork): endpoints, hooks, interfaces, displays, layouts, modules, panels, operations, bundles. Crucially: can you add (a) entirely custom UI pages/apps via **Modules**, (b) custom field input UIs via **Interfaces**, (c) custom list/board views via **Layouts**, and (d) custom server logic via **Hooks/Endpoints** — all WITHOUT forking core? Are extensions hot-loaded from an extensions folder / Marketplace at runtime?

3. **The Directus Marketplace + ready-made skins/templates.** Search the Marketplace and community for: (a) any **PROJECT MANAGEMENT** template, skin, kanban/gantt layout extension, or starter that makes Directus usable as a PM tool; (b) any **CRM** template/skin/starter. List concrete ones you find with names + URLs + whether they're maintained/open-source. Also check "Directus Templates" (the CLI template system / directus-labs / directus-community / awesome-directus). If there is NO turnkey PM or CRM skin, say so plainly.

4. **Field-level permissions** — confirm Directus supports role-based per-field read/write permissions (hide a "pricing" field from a "designer" role) natively via policies. Cite.

5. **Building the end-user app on top.** Realistically, to give non-technical users (designers, sales) a friendly PM/CRM experience, what are the options? (a) use Data Studio as-is with custom Layouts/Interfaces/Insights; (b) build a custom front-end app (Next.js/Vue) against the REST/GraphQL API using the official SDK; (c) community starter kits. Which do real teams use? Are there official/community **starter front-ends** (e.g., directus + Next.js CRM/PM starters)?

6. **License & self-host** — current license (MSCL/BSL), free-tier thresholds, and any features gated behind paid in self-host (does field-level permissions / SSO / Flows / extensions cost extra?).

7. **Automation (Flows)** — confirm built-in no-code automation (event/webhook/schedule triggers → operations) for things like "on stage change, write a transition-history row" — without forking.

Return: (1) a clear statement of how much is config/extension vs how much UI you must build yourself, (2) a concrete list of any ready-made open-source PM and CRM skins/templates/starters (or a clear "none found, here's the closest"), (3) the honest bottom line on "can a 9-person team realistically run their CRM + PM on Directus without forking, and how much app-building does it require", (4) cited URLs.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Links: [{"title":"Self-Hosted Pricing | Directus on-premise solutions","url":"https://directus.io/pricing/self-hosted"},{"title":"MSCL License | Directus","url":"https://directus.io/bsl"},{"title":"Directus Review 2026: Pros, Cons, Pricing & Verdict | Efficient App","url":"https://efficient.app/apps/directus"},{"title":"Day 31: Directus – Open-Source Headless CMS and Backend-As-A-Service Platform – 7 Days of Docker - portalZINE.DE","url":"https://portalzine.de/day-31-directus-open-source-headless-cms-and-backend-as-a-service-platform-7-days-of-docker/"},{"title":"Pricing | Directus","url":"https://directus.com/pricing"},{"title":"Directus Self-Hosted: Understanding Your Licensing and Cost Landscape - Oreate AI Blog","url":"https://www.oreateai.com/blog/directus-selfhosted-understanding-your-licensing-and-cost-landscape/3ef3f5f5c86f0079b9194ba3ab994fbf"},{"title":"What is Directus?","url":"https://pangea.app/glossary/directus"},{"title":"GitHub - directus/directus: The flexible backend for all your projects 🐰 Turn your DB into a headless CMS, admin panels, or apps with a custom UI, instant APIs, auth & more.","url":"https://github.com/directus/directus"},{"title":"Changing Our License: One Year Later | Directus","url":"https://directus.io/blog/changing-our-license-one-year-later"},{"title":"Evolving Our License for Long-Term Sustainability | Directus","url":"https://directus.io/blog/directus-v12-license-change"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Directus licensing:

## License Type

Directus is licensed under the Monospace Sustainable Core License (MSCL) 1.0, a source-available license derived from the Fair Core License. Previously, Directus operated under the Business Source License (BSL) for the past three years, though with the launch of v12 in May, they evolved to the Monospace Sustainable Core License (MSCL).

## Free Self-Hosting for Organizations Under $5M Revenue

If you and your organization have less than $5M in total annual income (revenue and any funding), you do not need to pay for self-hosting Directus, which applies to all projects, including production and commercial projects.

Under $5M in annual revenue and fewer than 50 employees, organizations get a fully permissive license for free on self-hosted. This is offered through the Innovation Grant, where individuals and organizations under the threshold (under $5M in annual revenue and 50 employees) can use the Directus platform completely free.

## Additional Details

Even above those thresholds, you can still explore and build on Directus for free within the Core tier limits, and every version automatically becomes fully open source (GPLv3) after 4 years.

Links: [{"title":"Layouts | Directus Docs","url":"https://docs.directus.io/user-guide/content-module/layouts"},{"title":"Directus Spotlight: Layouts | Directus","url":"https://directus.com/resources/directus-feature-spotlight-layouts"},{"title":"Directus Spotlight: Layouts","url":"https://directus.io/blog/directus-feature-spotlight-layouts"},{"title":"Layouts","url":"https://directus.com/docs/guides/content/layouts"},{"title":"Efficient Data Management with Directus Layouts: Tables, Cards, Calendars, Maps — Eightify","url":"https://eightify.app/summary/technology-and-software/efficient-data-management-with-directus-layouts-tables-cards-calendars-maps"},{"title":"Explore your data with customizable layouts | Directus Features","url":"https://directus.com/features/content-layouts"},{"title":"Visualize Your Data Better with Directus Layouts: Tables, Cards, Calendars, Maps and Kanban - YouTube","url":"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FVbhA1skkso"},{"title":"Directus CMS Features: 50+ Tools for Data Management (2026)","url":"https://mobian.studio/directus-cms-features/"},{"title":"Using the Map Layout in Directus | Directus TV","url":"https://directus.io/tv/uncharted-territory/using-the-map-layout-in-directus"},{"title":"Directus Docs","url":"https://docs.directus.io/app/layouts.html"}]

Based on the search results, here's information about Directus Data Studio layouts:

## Available Layouts

Directus Core comes with five built-in layouts – Table, Cards, Maps and Calendar – and the Kanban layout is available in Directus Cloud.

## Layout Types and Features

**Table Layout:** Works best for traditional data browsing with rows and columns display with sorting, filtering, and inline editing, allowing content teams to customize which fields appear, set column widths, and save view presets.

**Card Layout:** Transforms data into visual cards—ideal for media-heavy content like product catalogs or team directories, with each card displaying featured images, titles, and custom field data in a responsive grid.

**Calendar Layout:** Maps any collection with date fields onto a calendar interface, making publishing schedules, event management, and content planning become visual instead of tabular. To use this layout, the collection needs at least one datetime field to set a start time, but ideally two datetime fields.

**Map Layout:** Plots geospatial data on interactive maps. The Map Layout requires the Collection have a Map Field configured.

**Kanban Layout:** Organizes collections by status or category with drag-and-drop functionality that moves items between columns, making project management and workflow tracking more intuitive.

## Common Features

All layouts support data operations like search, filter and add data; auto-refresh at various intervals; and import/export capabilities via CSV, JSON or XML.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Directus Marketplace offerings for project management and CRM templates:

## Directus Cloud Templates

Directus Cloud Templates are available for website CMS, CRM and eCommerce projects, with data models, permissions and flows pre-configured to get started quicker. The CRM template is set up for managing deals, activities, contacts, and more, and since it's Directus, you can totally customize it to fit your exact needs.

## Layout Options

Directus offers Table, Card, Calendar, Map, or Kanban layouts based on your data and needs. You can also make your own layouts for things like Gantt charts or seating plans.

The Gantt Chart Layout displays items in a collection in a gantt chart for project management and task management, where you can specify a label, start date, end date, and optionally a dependency field, with zoom options ranging from hourly to yearly granularity.

## Extensions & Marketplace

Extensions in the Directus Marketplace are community-maintained and published to the registry. Users can publish extensions to the Directus Marketplace via npm, with Directus hosting a registry that includes enhanced search/filtering and security features.

For starting a project, community-maintained Directus instance templates can help you jump start your next project using the directus-template-cli command line utility.

Links: [{"title":"Extensions Overview","url":"https://directus.com/docs/guides/extensions/overview"},{"title":"Extensions | Directus Docs","url":"https://directus.io/docs/tutorials/extensions"},{"title":"Extension System and Event Hooks | directus/directus | DeepWiki","url":"https://deepwiki.com/directus/directus/3.7-extension-system-and-event-hooks"},{"title":"Directus Docs","url":"https://directus.com/docs/api/extensions"},{"title":"Overview | Directus Docs","url":"https://docs.directus.io/guides/extensions.html"},{"title":"Customize anything with extensions","url":"https://directus.io/features/custom-extensions"},{"title":"Modules | Directus Docs","url":"https://directus.io/docs/guides/extensions/app-extensions/modules"},{"title":"Custom Extensions for Directus — Michael Toohig","url":"https://michaeltoohig.com/blog/directus-extensions/"},{"title":"Interfaces | Directus Docs","url":"https://directus.io/docs/guides/extensions/app-extensions/interfaces"},{"title":"Extensions | Directus TV","url":"https://directus.io/tv/directus-academy/extensions"}]

Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive overview of Directus extensions SDK types, modules, interfaces, displays, layouts, panels, hooks, endpoints, operations, and bundles:

## Extension Types

Directus supports nine extension types: 'interface', 'display', 'layout', 'module', 'panel', 'hook', 'endpoint', 'operation', and 'bundle'.

## Frontend UI Extensions

**Interfaces:** Interfaces are form inputs used primarily on the item page and are the primary way users interact with data inside of Directus.

**Displays:** Displays are small components that are used to display a single value throughout the Data Studio and receive a single value and any custom display options.

**Layouts:** Layouts allow for listing of items on collection pages and receive a collection, filters, searches, and any custom layout options.

**Panels:** Panels are the building-blocks you can add onto insights dashboards to create, save and display data analytics.

**Modules:** Modules contain properties that control how a module is displayed in the module bar, the routes that exist within the module, and the actual Vue component that will be loaded.

## Backend API Extensions

**Hooks:** Hooks allow running code when events occur within Directus, with events triggered on schedules, database events, or during the Directus application lifecycle.

**Endpoints:** Endpoints allow you to register new API routes in your Directus project.

**Operations:** Operations are single steps in a Flow - the no-code automation tool part of Flows.

## Bundles

The 8 extension types can be combined to create even greater customizations, which is described in the docs as an extension bundle.

## Development

Extensions are built with Vue 3 and can use composables exported by the @directus/extensions-sdk package, and can be written in JavaScript or TypeScript.

input.prompt: List every template available in this repo. Which are CRM, project management, or business-app templates? Are they open source and maintained? What does each include (data model, flows, permissions)?

content.content:
# Directus Templates Available

Based on the repository, there are **5 templates**:

1. **AgencyOS** - Business/Agency management application
2. **Help Center** - Documentation/support system
3. **Simple CRM** - Customer relationship management
4. **Simple Ecommerce** - E-commerce platform
5. **Simple Website CMS** - Content management system

## Open Source & Maintenance Status

The repository describes itself as "Community maintained templates." All templates appear to be open source (hosted on GitHub under directus-labs), though specific maintenance levels aren't detailed in the provided content.

## What's Included

According to the documentation, templates contain:

- **Data Models**: "Schema Snapshot, Collections, Fields, Relations"
- **Access Control**: "Users, Roles, Permissions, Presets"
- **Automation**: "Flows, Operations"
- **Content Management**: "Dashboards, Panels"
- **Media Management**: "Folders, Files, Actual Files for Assets"
- **Sample Data**: "Translations, Content"

## CRM/Project Management Focus

Only **Simple CRM** explicitly targets CRM functionality. The others serve different business purposes (agency operations, help documentation, e-commerce, and website publishing) rather than project management specifically.

I found several relevant resources about Directus with Next.js starter templates. Here's what I found:

## Key Starter Templates

There's a Next.js 13 with App router starter web app with Directus as a headless CMS and Tailwind CSS for styling, available on GitHub at `fredygerman/next-js-directus-starter`. This includes examples for common uses like authentication, data fetching, web sockets, file uploads, and more.

Another comprehensive template is Nextus, a comprehensive, versatile and modern website template based on Next.js and Directus technologies that helps you build various types of websites more quickly. The Directus SDK is already integrated for you and available globally.

## Directus as a Headless CMS

Directus is an open-source low-code platform that allows you to create custom schemas, manage data, define roles & permissions, create workflows, analytics, and has strong capabilities in extensibility and flexibility - you can build anything from a simple headless CMS to a complex e-commerce platform or custom CRM.

## Official Documentation

The official Directus documentation provides tutorials on combining Directus (a headless CMS) with Next.js to streamline content management and create dynamic pages for a content management system. The Directus SDK (@directus/sdk) can be installed into Next.js projects for direct API integration.

The search results don't specifically mention a CRM-focused front-end SDK, but Directus is highly customizable and can be configured for CRM purposes.

Links: [{"title":"Access Control","url":"https://directus.com/docs/guides/auth/access-control"},{"title":"Directus Spotlight: Permissions and Access Control","url":"https://directus.io/blog/directus-feature-spotlight-permissions-and-access-control"},{"title":"Directus v11: Policies Permission System | Directus Blog","url":"https://directus.io/blog/v11-release-notes"},{"title":"Directus Roles and Permissions: How to Configure Access | OpsBlu Docs","url":"https://opsblu.com/documentation/website-platforms/directus/user-management/roles-permissions/"},{"title":"Permissions | Directus Docs","url":"https://docs.directus.io/reference/system/permissions"},{"title":"Directus Docs","url":"https://v8.docs.directus.io/guides/permissions.html"},{"title":"Permissions and Access Control | directus/directus | DeepWiki","url":"https://deepwiki.com/directus/directus/3.6-permissions-and-access-control"},{"title":"Granular permissions and access control","url":"https://directus.io/features/rule-based-access-control"},{"title":"Key User Management Concepts | Directus Docs","url":"https://docs.directus.io/user-guide/user-management/users-roles-permissions"},{"title":"Tutorials | Directus Docs","url":"https://docs.directus.io/blog/understanding-policy-based-access-control"}]

Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive overview of Directus field-level permissions, policies, and role-based access control:

## Policies and Permissions System

Directus 11 introduced a brand new permissions system based on policies, representing a significant shift in how Directus handles access control with more power and flexibility than ever before.

Policies are sets of rules that determine what users can and can't do in Directus projects, and they can be attached to users or roles, allowing for incredibly fine-grained control over permissions.

## Field-Level Access Control

You can now set permissions for specific fields, items, or even combinations of conditions. The system supports field-level permissions that filter accessible fields per user.

Fields are defined as CSV of fields that the user is allowed to interact with. The system supports explicitly denying read access for specific fields and explicitly denying write access for specific fields.

## Role-Based Access Control

The available actions in Directus are create, read, update, delete, and share. Roles can have any number of policies and child roles.

## Permission Inheritance and Merging

Policies don't override each other; instead they are additive, with permissions coming from all applied policies, meaning policies from a role or user are added together to get the combination of all permissions set in each policy.

This additive approach means that additional policies can only expand field access, never restrict it. Once a field is granted by any active policy, it remains accessible unless explicitly blocked by item-level permissions.

## Dynamic Variables

Policies can use dynamic variables like $CURRENT_USER or $CURRENT_ROLE, making it easy to create context-aware permissions that adapt to who's making the request.

Based on the search results, here's comprehensive information about Directus Flows:

## Overview

Flows enable custom, event-driven data processing and task automation within Directus. Each flow is composed of one trigger, a series of operations, and a data chain that is passed between each step.

## Trigger Types

There are 5 types of triggers: event hook, webhook, schedule (CRON), "another flow", and manual.

### Event Hooks
Event Hooks are triggered by events within the platform. The logic is based on Custom API Hooks. Event hooks come in two varieties:
- Filter (Blocking) — This pauses the transaction and passes the payload to your flow, allowing you to validate or transform payload or even prevent the event transaction.
- Action (Non-Blocking) — Actions do not block the event. A non-blocking action is mostly useful for completing tasks in response to an event, without modifying the event or slowing the API.

### Webhooks
Webhooks are triggered on an incoming HTTP request to: /flows/trigger/:this-webhook-trigger-id which you can copy from the webhook trigger panel. Method — Choose whether the Flow will be triggered by a GET or POST request from the dropdown. Asynchronous — Toggle whether or not the trigger responds asynchronously.

### Schedule (CRON)
This trigger type enables creating data at scheduled intervals, via 6-point cron job syntax. Interval — Set the cron job interval to schedule when the Flow triggers.

## Operations

They enable you to do things like manage data within Directus, transform the flow's data, send information off to outside services, set conditional logic, trigger other flows, and more.

## No-Code Interface

Directus Automate is extremely flexible and easy to configure. Using a simple no-code interface, you can connect any number of operations to create simple or complex workflows that execute automatically in response to a trigger.

# Extension Installation and Loading in Directus

The provided documentation doesn't explicitly detail installation mechanisms, folder structures, or hot-loading capabilities. However, it reveals key architectural insights:

## Installation Approach
The page mentions a "Directus Marketplace" and references a "CLI" for extensions, suggesting runtime installation without core modification. Extensions appear to be dropped-in rather than requiring source code forking.

## Execution Model
"Extensions in Directus run within the same environment as the main application, allowing them to leverage existing access to underlying services and UI components."

This shared-environment design indicates they're integrated at runtime rather than sandboxed.

## Extension Types

**App Extensions** (6 types):
- Interfaces, Displays, Layouts, Panels, Modules, Themes

**API Extensions** (3 types):
- Hooks, Endpoints, Operations

The documentation doesn't specify whether each type is sandboxed or operates as registered drop-ins. The shared-environment architecture suggests they're registered components rather than isolated sandboxes, but explicit confirmation requires additional sources.

**Note:** The security section warns that "Extensions execute code inside the Directus server," implying non-sandboxed execution—a critical distinction for trust and management policies.

# Directus Labs Extensions Analysis

## Project Management & CRM Extensions

**Gantt Chart Layout** - Found
- Package name: `gantt-chart-layout`
- Type: Layout
- Open source: Yes (MIT license)

**No Kanban Layout** - Not listed in the repository

## Additional Related Extensions

The repository contains several project-management-adjacent tools:

- **Timeline Chart Panel** (`timeline-chart-panel`) - Panel type
- **Table View Panel** (`table-view-panel`) - Panel type
- **Tree View Table Layout** (`tree-view-table-layout`) - Layout type

## Open Source Status

All extensions in this repository are published under the **MIT license**, making them open source. The repository itself states "Directus is a premium open-source (BSL 1.1) project," though these extensions use the more permissive MIT licensing.

## Summary

The Gantt Chart Layout is the primary project-management focused extension available. While other visualization and data management tools exist, there is no dedicated kanban layout or CRM-specific extensions listed in the current extension catalog.

content.content:
# AgencyOS Overview

## What is AgencyOS?

AgencyOS is "the open source operating system to help you run (or start) your digital agency." It combines Nuxt 3 frontend with Directus backend to provide a complete agency management solution.

## Tech Stack

**Frontend:** Nuxt 3 with Vue.js, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Nuxt UI, FormKit, and VueUse

**Backend:** Directus (headless CMS/data platform)

Language composition: 66.3% Vue, 31.6% TypeScript, 2% JavaScript

## Features

**Yes, it includes both CRM and project management:**
- Organizations, contacts, and sales pipeline
- Project and task management with customizable templates
- Client portal for self-service access
- Invoice and expense tracking
- Dashboard customization and Directus Flows automation

## Maintenance Status

Repository shows strong activity: 948 stars, 181 forks, 112 commits on main branch. However, the specific last commit date isn't visible in the provided content. Recent pull requests (4) and issues (19) indicate ongoing development.

## License

MIT license

## Architecture Notes

The frontend is a **custom Nuxt application** (not using Directus Data Studio). The project uses Directus as a backend via APIs rather than forking core—it applies the AgencyOS template through the Directus Template CLI, suggesting configuration-driven customization rather than core modifications.

# Directus Extension Kanboard Summary

**Purpose:** This is a kanban layout extension for Directus that allows users to customize kanban boards within the CMS interface.

**Installation:** It installs as a drop-in extension without requiring core modifications. Users can install via npm (`npm i directus-extension-kanboard`) or by compiling from source and copying files to the extensions directory.

**Maintenance Status:** The project shows limited recent activity. The latest release (v0.0.15) dates to January 4, 2024, with 14 total releases. The repository has modest engagement with 4 stars and 2 forks.

**License:** MIT license, permitting broad use and modification.

**Key Features:** The extension provides drag-and-drop card management, group organization, create/edit/delete operations for both cards and groups, changelog tracking, and customizable layout configuration through a sidebar panel.

**Tech Stack:** Built primarily with Vue (58.7%) and TypeScript (41.3%), indicating a modern, type-safe frontend implementation.

# Directus Self-Hosted: Free vs. Paid Features

## Self-Hosted Core (Free)
The Core tier includes "3 user seats, 25 Collections, 5 Flows, AI Assistant, Advanced RBAC" at no cost for self-hosting.

## Gated Behind Paid Tiers

**SSO/SAML**: Not available on Core. The page states "SSO is not available on Core and cannot be added as an add-on." This requires Team ($499/mo) or Enterprise.

**Field-Level Permissions**: Enterprise offers "Custom Access Policies," suggesting granular control is a premium feature, though Core includes "Advanced RBAC."

**Flows Automation**: Core includes 5 Flows; Team provides 20, and Enterprise offers "Unlimited Flows."

**Extensions**: The page doesn't explicitly detail extension availability by tier.

## Open Innovation Grant Eligibility
Organizations qualify if they meet both thresholds: "under $5M in annual revenue and fewer than 50 employees." Qualifying teams receive "fully permissive access to Directus at no cost" for self-hosted deployments, or $99/month for cloud hosting.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the Directus CRM cloud template:

## Directus Simple CRM Template

Directus offers a Simple CRM template that allows you to craft your own custom CRM with deals, activities, contacts and more. The Simple CRM template is powerful enough for serious business and simple enough to make it your own.

## Key Features

The template includes the essential CRM functionalities:
- Data models for contacts, organizations, deal pipelines, activities, and more - linked together with dynamic relationships
- A visual Kanban pipeline, filtered views, user assignments, and automated notifications upon deal creation

## Deployment via directus-template-cli

Templates are extracted and applied using the directus-template-cli command line utility. You can choose from community maintained templates or also choose a template from a local directory or a public GitHub repository.

To use the template on Directus Cloud, you need to:
1. Create a Directus instance on Directus Cloud or using self-hosted version, login and create a Static Access Token for the admin user, and copy the static token and your Directus URL.
2. Run the command on the terminal and follow the prompts.

PostgreSQL is the tested and preferred database vendor for templates.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the Directus Innovation Grant (called the "Open Innovation Grant"):

## Directus Open Innovation Grant

Organizations under $5M in annual revenue and 50 employees can use the Directus platform for free under the Open Innovation Grant— no strings attached.

### Key Features:

The grant includes unlimited seats, collections, and flows, SSO and custom access policies, and bring-your-own LLM and AI translations. Self-hosted: free, no caps.

The Open Innovation Grant gives qualifying teams full access to Directus at no cost. No feature limits. No artificial caps. Free to self-host.

### Eligibility:

Applicants must be under $5M annual recurring revenue and fewer than 50 employees.

### Hosting Options:

- Self-hosted is completely free.
- Cloud: pay only the hosting cost of $99/month

### Self-Hosted Specifics:

If you and your organization have less than $5M in total annual income (revenue and any funding), you do not need to pay for self-hosting Directus. This applies to all projects, including production and commercial projects.

You can apply for the grant at Directus's official Open Innovation Grant page (directus.com/oig).

content.text:
I have enough verified information to write the report. Let me compile the findings.

---

# Directus research — for a 9-person company deciding CRM + PM without forking

All claims below are from current (2024–2025) official Directus docs, GitHub, and the Directus Marketplace/templates. URLs cited throughout.

## 1. What Directus actually is

Directus is a **headless/back-end "data platform"** that wraps a SQL database (Postgres preferred; also MySQL, SQLite, etc.). It introspects an existing or new SQL schema and instantly gives you REST + GraphQL APIs, auth, and an admin app called **Data Studio**. It is **NOT a CRM or PM tool out of the box** — it is a generic data layer + admin UI. (https://github.com/directus/directus, https://directus.com/)

What stock Data Studio gives an end user:
- **Collections** (tables) and a configurable **item editor** (the per-record form).
- **Layouts** for browsing a collection: **Table, Cards, Calendar, Map, and Kanban**. Table/Cards/Calendar/Map are core; **Kanban is a built-in layout** (drag-and-drop between status columns). (https://docs.directus.io/user-guide/content-module/layouts, https://directus.com/features/content-layouts)
- **Insights** dashboards built from Panels (charts, metrics, lists).
- **Kanban: YES, built in.** **Gantt: NO, not built in** — it is available only as a community/Directus-Labs layout extension (see §3). (https://github.com/directus-labs/extensions/tree/main/packages/gantt-chart-layout)

Note: one third-party source claimed Kanban is "Cloud only," but the official layouts docs/features list Kanban as a standard layout. Treat Kanban as available in self-host. I could not find a definitive statement that self-host Kanban is gated; the Open Innovation Grant (§6) removes feature caps anyway.

## 2. Extensions SDK — every type, and whether it's a no-fork drop-in

Directus has **9 extension types**, split into App (UI) and API (server). **All are drop-ins — none require forking core.** (https://directus.com/docs/guides/extensions/overview)

**App / UI extensions (Vue 3):**
- **Interfaces** — custom field input UIs (your (b)). ✅ drop-in
- **Displays** — how a single value renders read-only.
- **Layouts** — custom collection list/board views, e.g. kanban/gantt (your (c)). ✅ drop-in
- **Panels** — Insights dashboard widgets.
- **Modules** — **entirely custom full-page apps/routes** in the admin nav bar (your (a)). ✅ drop-in
- **Themes** — styling.

**API / server extensions:**
- **Hooks** — run code on events (filter/blocking or action), schedules, lifecycle (your (d)). ✅ drop-in
- **Endpoints** — register new custom API routes (your (d)). ✅ drop-in
- **Operations** — custom steps for Flows automation.

**Bundles** — package several of the above together.

**Loading model:** Extensions are installed via the **Marketplace (npm registry)** or by dropping built files into the `./extensions` folder; they're **registered/hot-loaded at runtime** with no source modification. Important caveat: extensions "execute code inside the Directus server" and run in the same environment as the app — they are registered components, **not hardened sandboxes**, so you trust the code you install. (https://directus.com/docs/guides/extensions/overview, https://github.com/codihaus/directus-extension-kanboard — `npm i directus-extension-kanboard` then copy to extensions dir)

**Bottom line for §2:** Yes to all four — custom pages (Modules), custom field UIs (Interfaces), custom board/list views (Layouts), and custom server logic (Hooks/Endpoints) are all achievable **without forking core.**

## 3. Marketplace + ready-made skins/templates (the decisive section)

There are two distinct mechanisms: **extensions** (UI/logic add-ons) and **templates** (whole data-models + flows + permissions applied via `directus-template-cli`).

**Ready-made PM/agency app — the standout finding: AgencyOS**
- **AgencyOS** by Directus Labs — "open-source operating system for digital agencies." **Nuxt 3 front-end + Directus back-end.** Includes a **CRM (organizations, contacts, sales pipeline, activities), project + task management with project templates, a client portal, and invoice/expense tracking.** MIT licensed, ~948 stars, actively developed. The front-end is a **custom Nuxt app**, not Data Studio, and it uses Directus as backend via API + a template (config-driven, **no core fork**). This is the closest thing to a turnkey PM+CRM-on-Directus. (https://github.com/directus-labs/agency-os, https://directus.io/templates/agency-os)

**Official templates (applied via `directus-template-cli`, run inside Data Studio):**
- **Simple CRM** — deals, contacts, organizations, activities, **visual Kanban deal pipeline with drag-and-drop**, filtered views, user assignment, automated notification Flows. Runs **inside Data Studio** (no separate front-end required); config + flows, no fork. (https://directus.com/templates/simple-crm, https://github.com/directus-labs/directus-templates)
- Other templates: AgencyOS, Help Center, Simple Ecommerce, Simple Website CMS. (https://github.com/directus-labs/directus-templates)
- Templates package: schema/collections/fields/relations, users/roles/permissions/presets, flows/operations, dashboards/panels, sample data.

**PM-relevant layout/panel extensions (Directus Labs, MIT, drop-in):**
- **Gantt Chart Layout** (`gantt-chart-layout`) — Gantt with label/start/end/dependency fields, hourly-to-yearly zoom. (https://github.com/directus-labs/extensions/tree/main/packages/gantt-chart-layout)
- **Tree View Table Layout**, **Timeline Chart Panel**, **Table View Panel**, etc. (https://github.com/directus-labs/extensions)

**Kanban as an extension (in addition to the built-in layout):**
- `directus-extension-kanboard` (codihaus) — community kanban layout, MIT, but **lightly maintained** (last release v0.0.15, Jan 2024; ~4 stars). Built-in Kanban or Simple CRM's pipeline is the safer bet. (https://github.com/codihaus/directus-extension-kanboard)

**Honest gap:** Aside from **AgencyOS** (PM+CRM, custom Nuxt front-end) and the **Simple CRM** template (CRM in Data Studio), there is **no other turnkey, polished, "install-and-done" PM or CRM skin**. There is no standalone open-source "PM template that runs in Data Studio" with the depth of a Jira/Asana. The PM story in Data Studio is "use Kanban + Gantt-extension + Calendar layouts over your own task collections," not a pre-skinned product.

## 4. Field-level permissions — confirmed native

Yes. Since **Directus 11**, access control is **policy-based** and supports **per-field read/write permissions natively**. You can grant/deny specific fields per policy (CSV of allowed fields, explicit read-deny and write-deny), attach policies to roles/users, and use dynamic vars like `$CURRENT_USER`. So "hide the pricing field from the designer role" is standard config. One nuance: policies are **additive** (a field granted by any active policy stays granted unless blocked at item level), so design role/policy composition carefully. (https://directus.io/blog/v11-release-notes, https://directus.com/docs/guides/auth/access-control, https://directus.io/features/rule-based-access-control)

## 5. Building the end-user app on top

Three realistic options:

**(a) Use Data Studio as-is** — for internal users (sales, ops, designers), configure collections + Kanban/Calendar/Gantt layouts + custom Interfaces + Insights dashboards + per-field policies. This is the **lowest-effort path** and is what the Simple CRM template does. Good enough for many internal teams; the UI is the generic admin app, not a bespoke product.

**(b) Build a custom front-end** (Next.js or Nuxt/Vue) against REST/GraphQL using the **official `@directus/sdk`**. This is what you do when you need a polished, branded, opinionated UX for non-technical users. **AgencyOS is exactly this pattern** and gives you a real running PM+CRM front-end to start from.

**(c) Community starters:** AgencyOS (best — PM+CRM); plus general CMS starters (`fredygerman/next-js-directus-starter`, `nextus`, `14790897/directus-starter`). I could **not** find a maintained standalone "Directus + Next.js **CRM** starter" other than AgencyOS (which is Nuxt). (https://github.com/directus-labs/agency-os, https://github.com/fredygerman/next-js-directus-starter, https://github.com/luochuanyuewu/nextus)

Real teams typically do (a) for internal back-office and (b) when they need a customer/end-user-facing experience.

## 6. License & self-host (decisive for this company)

- **License:** As of **v12 (May 2025)**, Directus moved from BSL 1.1 to the **Monospace Sustainable Core License (MSCL) 1.0** (source-available; derived from Fair Core License). Each version becomes **GPLv3 after 4 years.** (https://directus.io/blog/directus-v12-license-change, https://directus.com/mscl)
- **Open Innovation Grant** (the relevant tier here): organizations **under $5M annual revenue AND under 50 employees** get **"unlimited seats, collections, and flows, SSO and custom access policies… Self-hosted: free, no caps."** You must apply/agree to the grant terms. (https://directus.com/oig)
- **Without the grant**, the free self-host **Core** tier is capped (a third-party reading of the pricing page cited ~3 users / 25 collections / 5 Flows; **SSO and "Custom Access Policies" are paid Team/Enterprise features**). (https://directus.com/pricing)
- **Net for this company:** A 9-person, <$5M company **qualifies for the Open Innovation Grant**, which **removes all caps and unlocks SSO + custom access policies + unlimited flows, free, self-hosted.** So in practice **field-level permissions, SSO, Flows, and extensions are all free for them** — but this depends on **applying for and being granted the OIG**, not on the default Core tier. That dependency is worth flagging in the decision.

## 7. Automation (Flows) — confirmed, no fork

**Directus Automate / Flows** is built-in no-code automation: **one trigger + chained operations.** Trigger types: **Event hook** (filter/blocking or action/non-blocking on DB events), **Webhook** (GET/POST), **Schedule (CRON)**, **Another flow**, and **Manual.** Operations can read/write Directus data, transform data, call external services, branch on conditions, run custom **Operation** extensions, etc. So "on stage change → write a transition-history row" is a standard **Event-hook (action) Flow → Create Item operation** — pure config, no fork. (https://directus.io/docs/guides/automate/flows, https://directus.io/docs/guides/automate/triggers, https://directus.io/docs/guides/automate/operations)

---

# Synthesis / bottom line

**(1) How much is config/extension vs build-yourself.** The **entire data model, permissions (incl. field-level), automation (Flows), APIs, auth, and admin UI (Table/Cards/Kanban/Calendar/Map + Insights) are config — zero code, zero fork.** Gaps you fill with **drop-in extensions** (Gantt layout, custom field Interfaces, custom Modules/pages, custom Hooks/Endpoints) — still **no fork.** What you actually *build* is only: (i) any **bespoke end-user front-end** beyond the admin app, and (ii) any niche custom extension not already published. The forking pain they hate with Twenty CRM essentially **does not exist here** — Directus's whole extension model is designed to avoid touching core.

**(2) Ready-made open-source PM/CRM skins/templates/starters:**
- **AgencyOS** (MIT, Nuxt + Directus) — full **CRM + project/task management + client portal**, the strongest turnkey starter. https://github.com/directus-labs/agency-os
- **Simple CRM** template (CRM with Kanban pipeline, runs in Data Studio). https://directus.com/templates/simple-crm
- **Gantt Chart Layout** extension (MIT). https://github.com/directus-labs/extensions/tree/main/packages/gantt-chart-layout
- Built-in **Kanban/Calendar/Map** layouts; lightly-maintained `directus-extension-kanboard`.
- **No** other deep, polished standalone PM product-skin exists. For PM specifically, AgencyOS is the closest turnkey thing; otherwise you assemble Kanban+Gantt+Calendar over your own task collections.

**(3) Can a 9-person team realistically run CRM + PM on Directus without forking?** **Yes, realistically — and without forking.** Path of least resistance: apply for the **Open Innovation Grant** (they qualify), stand up Directus, apply the **Simple CRM template** and/or fork-clone **AgencyOS**, model your PM tasks, add the **Gantt extension**, wire **Flows** for automations, and lock down **field-level policies**. App-building effort is **low-to-moderate**: near-zero if internal users live in Data Studio; moderate if you want a branded customer-facing front-end (AgencyOS gives you a big head start there). The realistic build surface is the front-end UX and a handful of custom Interfaces/Modules — not core modifications. **Two honest caveats:** (a) the "free everything" depends on **being granted the OIG** (default Core tier is capped and gates SSO/custom policies); (b) extensions run **in-process, not sandboxed**, so vet what you install.
